22: 2 Chronicles ends with Cyrus's decree (536 BCE) permitting the Jewish exiles to return and rebuild the temple — the same decree that opens Ezra. This ending was the Chronicler's editorial choice: rather than ending with Jerusalem's destruction (as Kings does), Chronicles ends with the first words of restoration. The last word of the Hebrew canon (as traditionally ordered) is this: 'Whoever is among you of all his people, may the LORD his God be with him. Let him go up.' The Chronicler makes the exile the penultimate chapter, not the final one; the return from exile is YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant promise. The NT reads the exile-and-return pattern as a type of death-and-resurrection: the people 'died' in Babylon and were 'raised' in the return; Christ dies and rises as the ultimate exile-and-return.
1: “The people of the land” (ʿam hāʾāreṣ) — a specific sociological group in Chronicles designating the landed nobility or free citizenry of Judah, distinct from the royal household. They exercise popular sovereignty to choose Josiah’s successor, bypassing formal succession. They select Jehoahaz rather than his older brother Eliakim (v4) — likely because Jehoahaz was seen as more resistant to Egyptian control. This popular assertion of sovereignty in the succession reflects the ʿam hāʾāreṣ’s role in making (and sometimes breaking) kings in the final decades of Judah (cf. 2 Kgs 11:14-20; 21:24; 23:30).
2: Jehoahaz’s three-month reign — the second shortest in the Davidic dynasty (Joash reigned three months in 2 Kgs 11, but as a child under Athaliah). The Chronicler gives no evaluation of Jehoahaz’s character (contrast 2 Kgs 23:32: “he did what was evil”). Chronicles moves quickly through the last four kings because their reigns represent the working out of the accumulated sin narrated in the preceding chapters — each king merely confirms the trajectory already set.
3: Neco deposes Jehoahaz and imposes tribute: 100 talents of silver and one talent of gold. The imposition of tribute signals Judah’s reduction to vassal status under Egypt. The Davidic king is now subject to foreign power — a reversal of the covenant promise that the nations would be tributary to David’s heir. The precise tribute amounts echo the Chronicler’s concern for historical specificity; these figures also appear in 2 Kgs 23:33.
4: Neco renames Eliakim as Jehoiakim — renaming a vassal king was a standard ancient Near Eastern practice asserting the suzerain’s authority over the vassal’s identity. Both names contain divine elements (Eliakim = “God raises up”; Jehoiakim = “YHWH raises up”), so the name change here is more about political submission than theological substitution. Jehoahaz is taken to Egypt and dies there — his fate fulfills Jeremiah’s prophecy (Jer 22:11-12: “he shall never return here”).
5: Jehoiakim’s 11-year reign (609-598 BCE). He is the king who burned Jeremiah’s scroll (Jer 36:23) and who Jeremiah said would have the “burial of a donkey” (Jer 22:19). The Chronicler gives only the standard negative evaluation; Kings gives more detail (2 Kgs 23:35-24:5). Jehoiakim shifts allegiance between Egypt and Babylon multiple times during his reign as the geopolitical situation changes — he is a king whose policies are driven by calculation rather than covenant faithfulness.
6: Nebuchadnezzar comes and binds Jehoiakim in bronze chains (baḥuštatayim) to take him to Babylon. This is the first Babylonian intervention in Judah (605 BCE, after the Battle of Carchemish). The historicity of Jehoiakim actually being taken to Babylon is debated — 2 Kings says he died in Jerusalem. The Chronicler may be presenting a partial submission (Nebuchadnezzar came and subjugated him, possibly taking him briefly and releasing him as a vassal) or using the pattern of temple vessels removed to Babylon (v7) as the narrative framework regardless of what happened to the king personally.
7: Temple vessels (mîkᵉlê bêt YHWH) — great and small — begin their journey to Babylon. Nebuchadnezzar places them in his temple (bêtô) in Babylon. This is the Chronicler’s central concern in ch 36: the progressive desecration and removal of the temple vessels is the narrative thread connecting all four kings. The vessels that Solomon had dedicated (chs 5-7) are systematically stripped; when the temple itself is finally burned (v19), nothing sacred remains. Dan 1:2 notes that vessels were placed in the treasury of Nebuchadnezzar’s god — the reverse of temple dedication.
8: “What was found against him” (hannimṣāʾ ʿālāyw) — an enigmatic phrase, possibly referring to offenses documented in official records. The source citation points to the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah. Jehoiakim is the only king for whom this specific phrase appears — it may indicate a judicial record of crimes brought against him, consistent with Jeremiah’s extensive indictment of his reign (Jer 22-26; 36).
9: Jehoiachin’s age is given as 8 in Chronicles versus 18 in 2 Kgs 24:8 — a well-known textual discrepancy; most textual critics treat the number 8 (šᵉmōneh) as a scribal error for 18 (šᵉmōneh ʿeśrēh). The three months and ten days is an oddly precise figure suggesting use of official records. Like his grandfather Josiah at age 8 (34:1), a very young king of Judah faces enormous political pressure — but Jehoiachin’s reign ends in exile, not reform.
10: “At the turn of the year” (lᵉtᵉšûḇat haššānāh) — in spring (the agricultural/civil new year). Nebuchadnezzar sends for Jehoiachin (March-April 597 BCE) and takes him to Babylon along with more precious vessels. His uncle Mattaniah is renamed Zedekiah by Nebuchadnezzar — the second renaming by a foreign power in this chapter, again asserting vassalage through naming authority. Zedekiah is thus king not by popular choice (like Jehoahaz) but by Babylonian appointment — his legitimacy is entirely external.
11: Zedekiah’s 11-year reign (597-586 BCE) — the longest of the four final kings. He is the last king of Judah to sit on David’s throne in Jerusalem, making him the tragic terminus of the Davidic monarchy as an earthly institution. Chronicles abbreviates Zedekiah’s reign drastically (compared to the extended treatment in Jer 27-44 and 2 Kgs 24:18-25:7), focusing entirely on the reasons for the exile rather than the events of the siege.
12: Zedekiah’s defining failure: he did not humble himself (lōʾ kānaʿ) before Jeremiah the prophet. The verb kānaʿ (humble oneself) is the Chronicler’s central diagnostic term for the covenant response that averts judgment: Rehoboam humbled himself and the catastrophe was limited (12:12); Manasseh humbled himself and was restored (33:12, 19, 23). Zedekiah’s failure to humble himself before the prophet who spoke from YHWH’s mouth is the decisive act that seals Jerusalem’s fate. He also does not humble himself before Nebuchadnezzar (v13) — both political and prophetic submission are withheld.
13: Zedekiah’s rebellion against Nebuchadnezzar — who had exacted an oath in God’s name (ʾᵃšer hišbîʿô bēʾlōhîm) — is therefore also a breach of a covenant oath. The double hardening formula: qāšāh ʿōrep (stiffened his neck) + ʾimmaṣ ʾet-lᵉḇāḇô (hardened his heart) against turning to YHWH, the God of Israel. The Chronicler never says this of any previous king — the hardening of Pharaoh (Exod 4:21) uses identical vocabulary, casting Zedekiah’s hardening as a Pharaoh-level resistance to divine word. The result, as with Pharaoh, is total catastrophe.
14: The indictment expands to encompass the entire leadership: the chief priests and the people also multiplied their maʿal (treachery/unfaithfulness). The key term maʿal — the Chronicler’s diagnostic word for covenant betrayal that appears throughout the book (from Achan in Joshua to Saul in 1 Chr 10:13) — reaches its intensified form here: hirḇû limaʿôl maʿal (they greatly multiplied treachery). The priests following “the abominations of the nations” is the ultimate betrayal — those charged with guarding covenant boundaries have adopted the very practices that caused the Canaanites to lose the land (Lev 18:24-28).
15: YHWH’s persistent sending of messengers — “rising early and sending” (hašḵēm wᵉšālôaḥ) — is the characteristic Jeremianic idiom for relentless prophetic warning (Jer 7:13, 25; 11:7; 25:3-4; 26:5; 29:19; 32:33; 35:14-15; 44:4). The Chronicler adopts this language to characterize YHWH’s persistence: because he had compassion (ḥāmal) on his people and his dwelling place. The motivation is ḥāmal — the same verb used for a father’s pity for his children. The exile is not YHWH’s first response but his last, after long and compassionate warning.
16: The verdict on the final generation uses three verbs of prophetic rejection: mocking the messengers (bᵉmaʿᵃlê malʾᵃḵê hāʾĕlōhîm — mocking at them), despising his words (ûmᵉbazzîm dᵉḇārāyw), scoffing at his prophets (ûmitôlᵉlîm bînᵉḇîʾāyw). The wrath of YHWH rose (wayyaʿal) against his people until there was lēʾên marpēʾ (no remedy, no healing). Marpēʾ is the word for “healing” or “remedy” (from rāpāʾ); the prefix lēʾên negates it absolutely — there is no medicine left, no cure possible. This is the terminal diagnosis of a patient who refused every treatment. The Chronicler’s most devastating verdict is medical, not merely judicial.
17: YHWH brings the king of the Chaldeans against them. The text specifies that Nebuchadnezzar killed the young men with the sword in the house of the sanctuary — not just in the city but in the temple precincts themselves. No compassion is shown for young men, women, or the elderly — the comprehensive formula of Deut 28:50 (“a nation of fierce countenance that will show no regard for the elderly or favor to the young”) is enacted. YHWH gave them all into his hand — the passive divine causation underlying the Babylonian agency is explicit: this is not random conquest but covenantally-authorized judgment.
18: The temple vessels — great and small, including the treasures of the temple and of the king and his officers — are all taken to Babylon. The comprehensive stripping of the temple is the inverse of Solomon’s dedication: what was accumulated over centuries is removed in days. The vessels will eventually be returned under Cyrus (Ezra 1:7-11) — their restoration will be the first tangible sign of the exile’s reversal. The Chronicler tracks the vessels as a proxy for the covenant: when they are in Jerusalem, YHWH dwells with his people; when they are in Babylon, the covenant bond is suspended.
19: The destruction triad: they burned the house of God, broke down the walls of Jerusalem, burned all its palaces, and destroyed all its precious objects. The temple — Solomon’s temple, the subject of the Chronicler’s narrative from 1 Chr 17 onward — is reduced to ash. The burning of bêt hāʾĕlōhîm is the Chronicler’s most catastrophic event. The walls’ destruction strips Jerusalem of defensive identity; the palace fires erase the Davidic royal presence. The Hebrew phrase wayyašḥîtû ʾet kol-kᵉlê maḥᵃmaddeyāh (they destroyed all its precious objects) echoes Lamentations’ maḥᵃmaddeyāh — the precious things mourned in Lam 1:7, 10-11.
20: The survivors are taken into exile in Babylon as servants (lᵃḇāḏîm) — the word for slavery. From “sons of Abraham” and “holy nation” (Exod 19:6) to slaves in a foreign empire. “Until the kingdom of Persia came to power” — the mention of Persia here anticipates the resolution that will come through Cyrus in vv 22-23. The exile is named as temporary from the outset of the exile narrative: its terminus is already defined before the reader can despair.
21: The theological explanation of the exile: it fulfilled the word of YHWH through Jeremiah (Jer 25:11; 29:10 — seventy years for Babylon’s dominance), “until the land had paid off its Sabbaths” (ʿad-rāṣᵉtāh hāʾāreṣ ʾet-šabbᵉtōtêhā). Leviticus 26:34-35 had warned that if Israel failed to observe the sabbatical years (every seventh year the land rests, Lev 25:1-7), the land would “enjoy its Sabbaths while it lies desolate.” The exile is understood as the land reclaiming its accumulated Sabbath rest that Israel had stolen from it by continuous cultivation. The Chronicler counts the exile not as punishment alone but as the land’s restoration — YHWH enforces the Sabbath economics that Israel refused to implement voluntarily. The seventy years = approximately 490 years of violated sabbatical rest (70 × 7) since entering Canaan.
23: Cyrus’s proclamation — the actual text of the decree as issued. “YHWH the God of heaven has given me all the kingdoms of the earth, and he has charged me to build him a house in Jerusalem” — a Persian king acknowledging Israel’s God as the source of his empire is one of the most astonishing sentences in the Hebrew Bible. Isaiah had named Cyrus 150 years earlier (Isa 44:28; 45:1) and called him YHWH’s mᵉšîaḥ (anointed). The final word of Chronicles — and of the Hebrew Bible in its canonical order — is wᵉyaʿal: “let him go up.” The Hebrew canon closes not with an ending but with an imperative of ascent. The exile that began with the descent to Babylon ends with the divine summons to go up to Jerusalem — the return anticipated by Isaiah (Isa 40:3-5, the highway from Babylon to Zion) and enacted by Ezra-Nehemiah. This is the canonical seam between Chronicles and Ezra, deliberately left open rather than closed.
